mnemonics: DISTANCE: A Metric must be Definite, Identity of indiscernibles, Symmetric, Triangle Inequality, Always non-negative, Continuous space foundation, Every pair of points measured
keyPoints: A metric space is a set X with a distance function d: X × X → ℝ satisfying: 1) d(x,y) ≥ 0, 2) d(x,y) = 0 iff x=y, 3) d(x,y) = d(y,x), 4) d(x,z) ≤ d(x,y) + d(y,z)
Common examples include: ℝⁿ with Euclidean distance, any set with discrete metric (0 if x=y, 1 otherwise), function spaces with supremum norm
The metric topology defines open sets as unions of open balls B(x,ε) = {y ∈ X : d(x,y) < ε}
commonMistakes: Assuming all metrics satisfy d(x,y) ≤ 1 (false - Euclidean distance can be arbitrarily large)
Confusing metric spaces with topological spaces (all metric spaces are topological, but not vice versa)
Forgetting to verify the triangle inequality when defining a new metric
